---
title: "The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation"
description: "When a model is numerically expensive, Julia runs it fast — inside the same document, beside R and Python."
image: thumbnail.svg
image-alt: "The Julia engine — speed for heavy computation — Blood Doctor · Quarto"
date: "2026-07-19"
categories: [Engines, Reproducibility]
engine: markdown
---

Some problems — large matrix differential equations, Monte-Carlo simulations,
population PK models — are simply slow in interpreted languages. **Julia** compiles
to native code and runs close to C speed, and Quarto speaks it natively.

## Same document, a third language

````markdown
```{julia}
using Plots
gr()
t = 0:0.5:48
C = 20.0 .* exp.(-(log(2)/12) .* t)
plot(t, C, lw = 2.4, color = :firebrick, xlabel = "Time (h)")
```
````

The remarkable part is not Julia alone — it is that an R chunk, a Python chunk and
a Julia chunk can sit in **one source file**, each doing what it is best at, and
render into one seamless document.

::: {.callout-tip}
## See it running live
The [**Julia engine page**](/engines/julia.qmd) executes a `Plots.jl` computation
at render time. The project's Julia environment is pinned via `Project.toml` /
`Manifest.toml` so the result is fully reproducible.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
Julia's first render compiles its packages and can take a minute; Quarto's
`freeze` then caches the result so subsequent builds are instant.
:::
